content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES C0M1’vlIssIoN
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
July 20, 1999
Robert K. Booth
Atkinson Farasyn, LLP
660 West Dana Street
Post Office Box 279
Mountain View, California 94042
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-99-193
Dear Mr. Booth:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the San Lorenzo Valley
Water District regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).
1 Since you have
not provided the name of the official whose duties are in question, we are treating your request as
one for informal assistance.
2 (Regulation 1 8329(b)(2)(A).)
QUESTION
A director of the San Lorenzo Valley Water District lives and operates a winery adjacent
to watershed property owned by the district. May he participate in a decision regarding the
future low intensity use of the district’s property?
CONCLUSION
No. The director may not make, participate in making, or use his official position to
influence the decision, since it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have some
financial effect on his adjacent property.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the immunity conferred by formal written advice. (Regulation
1 8329(c)(3).)


File No. 1-99-193
Page 2
FACTS
You are the general counsel for the San Lorenzo Valley Water District (“district”). An
elected member of the district owns 26 acres ofproperty upon which he and his spouse reside in
their home, and upon which he grows grapes and operates a small winery. The boundary ofhis
acreage is contiguous with a 1,350 acre watershed owned by the district. The district’s property
is largely undeveloped and has been kept in a primitive state for many years. That property also
shares a boundary with Castle Rock State Park, a unit of the California Park System.
For some time, the district has been considering the future of its property. Among the
uses being considered are sustainable yield lumber harvests, which might be visible from the
director’s property. This use would be of short duration and conducted in a fashion so as to be
sensitive to the property itself, as well as to neighboring property. Other alternatives include
selling the property to a private nonprofit group that preserves open space; transferring the
property to the State Park System; or operating a private nonprofit educational program that
emphasizes environmentally sensitive forest uses, such as furniture making and similar low
intensity uses. The district is not considering constructing any commercial enterprises of any
type that involves intensive grading, paving or construction on the property, or other
traffic-generating uses. However, it is possible that a low-intensity or walk-in campground
might be operated on the property under one of the scenarios outlined above.
With your request, you provided a written opinion from Frank 0. May, M.A.L, a certified
real estate appraiser located in Santa Cru.z County. Mr. May believes that any uses of the
property discussed above would have a minimal impact on the director’s holdings.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or in any way
attempting to use their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have
a financial interest. (Section 87100.) A member of a water district is a public official for
purposes of the Act. (Section 82048.)
Identifying Economic Interests
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on, among other enumerated economic interests: (1) any business
entity in which the official has an investment interest worth $1,000 or more; (2) any interest in
real property worth $1,000 or more; (3) any source of income of $250 provided to the official
within 12 months prior to the time the decision is made; or (4) any business entity in which the
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or holds any position ofmanagement.
(Section 871 03(a)-(d).)


File No. 1-99-193
Page 3
The director operates a small winery. Presumably, he has an investment interest in his
winery worth $1,000 or more, and the winery is a source of income to him of $250 or more
within the previous 12 months. (Section 87 103(a) and (c).) In addition, as the owner ofthe
winery, he has an economic interest in the winery pursuant to Section 87 103(d).
The director also owns 26 acres ofproperty on which the winery and his personal
residence are located. Presumably, this property is worth $1,000 or more.
Accordingly, the director may not make, participate in making, or use his official position
to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on his winery or on his 26-acre property.
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
the official’s economic interest?
Once the director identifies his economic interests, he must evaluate whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on that economic
interest. First, he must determine whether the economic interest will be directly or indirectly
involved in the decision. Based upon the type of involvement, he must then locate the
appropriate materiality standard set forth in Commission regulations. After finding the relevant
materiality standard, he must decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the materiality
standard will be met.
1. Direct or Indirect Involvement
The Winery
A business entity is directly involved in a decision if the entity is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18704.1 (a)(2).) An entity is the subject of a decision if the
decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit or
contract with the person. (Id.) If a business entity is not directly involved in the decision, it is
indirectly involved for purposes of locating the relevant materiality standard. (Regulation
18704.1(b).) For materiality purposes, the director’s winery is indirectly involved in the decision
regarding the use of the district’s watershed property.
The Property
Real property is directly involved in a decision if the decision involves, among other
things: (1) the zoning of such property; (2) or a license or permit authorizing a specific use of
such property. (Regulation 18704.2.) Ifreal property is not directly involved in a decision, it is
indirectly involved for purposes of applying the materiality standards. (Regulation 18704.2(b).)


File No. 1-99-193
Page 4
For materiality purposes, the director’s property is indirectly involved in the decision
regarding the use of the district’s watershed property.
2. Applicable Materiality Standard
The Wineiy
When a business entity is indirectly involved in a decision, the pertinent materiality
standard is set forth in Regulation 18705.1(b). The materiality thresholds in the regulation vary
depending upon the size of the business entity. Since you have not indicated the size ofthe
winery, you must look at Regulation 18705.1(b) to determine which standard will apply. For
relatively small businesses indirectly involved in a decision, the effect of a decision is material if
it will result in an increase or decreae in: (1) gross revenues of $10,000 or more in a fiscal year;
(2) existing expenses of $2,500 or more in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more. (Regulation 18705.1(b)(7).)
The Property
For real property indirectly involved in a decision, the applicable materiality standard
may be found in Regulation 18705.2(b). The materiality thresholds vary depending upon the
distance between the official’s real property and the real property that is the subject of the
decision. The boundaries of the director’s property is contiguous with the boundaries ofthe
district’s property. If an official’s real property is located within 300 feet from the property that
is the subject of a decision, the effect ofthe decision is deemed to be material, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the real property. (Regulation 1 8705.2(b)(1)(A).)
3. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. (Regulation 18706.) A material financial effect need not be a certainty as a result of
the decision, but it must be more than a mere possibility.
The Winery
Applying the above analysis, the critical question is whether it is substantially likely that
the decision will affect the director’s winery by the pertinent threshold amount in Regulation
18705.1(b). In his appraisal, Mr. May suggested that heavier traffic in the area as a result ofthe
decision could lead to an increase in wine sales. Assuming that the winery is a relatively small
business, if it is substantially likely that the decision will affect its gross revenues by $10,000 or
more in a fiscal year, its existing expenses by $2,500 or more in a fiscal year, or the current value
of its assets and liabilities by $10,000 or more, then the director will have a conflict of interest.


File No. 1-99-193
Page 5
The Property
When the effect of a decision is deemed to be material under the pertinent materiality
standard, the official must evaluate whether it is substantially likely that any financial effect will
occur as a result ofthe decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).) This is a “one-penny” rule—if any
financial effect is reasonably foreseeable, the official will have a conflict of interest in the
decision. Under the applicable materiality standard described above, the effect of the decision on
the director’s property is deemed to be material. Therefore, the director will have a conflict of
interest unless the decision will have absolutely no financial effect on the property.
In the appraisal, Mr. May stated: “It would be foolish to make the statement that the
Board Member’s Property would absolutely not be affected by the adjacent use, because adjacent
properties can have a substantial impact on the value pf a property.” He continued: “The general
land use density in the area is so low and surrounding parcel sizes are so large that any legally
permissible land use on a parcel would have a relatively insignificant impact on an adjacent
parcel.” He further declared that: “A noticeable change in market value to the Board Member’s
property as a direct result of any permissible adjacent land use is highly unlikely.”
Although the appraisal establishes that the effect ofthe decision on the director’s property
is minimal, the test is whether there will be any financial effect. Based on the statements made in
the appraisal, it appears that some, albeit minimal, financial effect on the property is substantially
likely. As such, the director will be disqualified from participating in any decision regarding the
future use of the district’s watershed property.
Please note that for conflict ofinterest purposes, the effect of a decision on each
economic interest held by an official must be analyzed independently. Thus, an effect on one
economic interest cannot be used to offset the effect on another economic interest to avoid the
disqualification requirements ofthe Act.
If you have other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

A director of the San Lorenzo Valley Water District lives and operates a winery adjacent
to watershed property owned by the district. May he participate in a decision regarding the
future low intensity use of the district’s property?

influence the decision, since it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have some
financial effect on his adjacent property.2 Informal assistance does not provide the immunity conferred by formal written advice. (Regulation
1 8329(c)(3).)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A director of the San Lorenzo Valley Water District lives and operates a winery adjacent
to watershed property owned by the district. May he participate in a decision regarding the
future low intensity use of the district’s property?

CONCLUSION: No. The director may not make, participate in making, or use his official position to
influence the decision, since it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have some
financial effect on his adjacent property.2 Informal assistance does not provide the immunity conferred by formal written advice. (Regulation
1 8329(c)(3).)
